input_path: data\papers\Mertens and Denil - 2025 - Lab-Scale Gantry Crane Digital Twin Exemplar.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: Lab‑scale gantry crane comprising a mechanical gantry structure equipped with motorized rails and an electronic controller; operating primarily in a laboratory environment, the crane is capable of controlled vertical and horizontal payload transport. Its articulated kinematic architecture, precise motion control, and measurable load handling make it an ideal candidate for a digital twin that can accurately simulate and monitor real‑time performance.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The digital twin delivers four core services: (1) optimal control of the crane’s motion trajectories derived from the kinematic model; (2) continuous real‑time logging of operational data from the physical system for historical analysis; (3) data visualization dashboards that render the crane’s states and performance metrics; and (4) continuous validation that compares live sensor data against the model’s predictions to detect deviations and potential faults.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The implementation relies on a stack of free and widely used open‑source components: a CAD model created in Autodesk Fusion 360, a kinematic description encoded in a Python simulation engine, communication handled by the Eclipse Mosquitto MQTT broker, persistent storage in a TimescaleDB time‑series database, presentation via Grafana dashboards, and Docker containerisation for reproducibility. The physical side is driven by an Arduino microcontroller that samples sensor currents and encoders, publishing data to MQTT, and concurrently receives control commands generated by the trajectory service.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The twin is designed for continuous, near‑real‑time operation: telemetry is ingested from the physical crane interface, transmitted via an MQTT broker to the twin, and compared against expected ranges in real time. No explicit periodic sampling frequency or latency bound is specified in the documentation; instead, the system processes data event‑driven as it arrives, thereby providing a dynamic, continuous time scale rather than a fixed update interval.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The system follows a component‑oriented software architecture composed of four core Python services: Telemetry (collecting sensor outputs from the physical crane via MQTT), Command (sending motor commands), Logging (writing captured states to a TimescaleDB database), and Validation (continuously comparing recorded state against expected trajectory). The services interconnect over an MQTT broker running on the same PC, and all persistence is handled by a TimescaleDB Post‑gres instance. The system is deployed directly on a personal computer without containerization or virtualization; it relies on open‑source tools such as Python, MQTT, TimescaleDB, and Grafana for visualization.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: The twin operates in a closed, local environment. All communication occurs over a local MQTT broker; there are no integrations with external digital twin frameworks, cloud services, or other enterprise systems. The design purposefully limits integration scope to local deployments, omitting any external data exchange protocols or interoperability standards beyond MQTT.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: GreenhouseDT implements a behavioral adaptation digital‑twin model that exchanges real‑time data with the physical greenhouse via an ActiveMQ message broker and persists measurements in an InfluxDB time‑series database. The twin is deployed inside virtual machine images that include prerecorded data for experimentation. The Air Quality Management exemplar constructs its twin from AutomationML model files; sensor data collected by a Raspberry‑Pi are transmitted through Microsoft Azure services, enabling runtime visualization, simulation, and prediction. Both exemplars rely on containerization or virtualization to package the twin components, while the underlying data flow is orchestrated by the broker and database layers.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Both exemplars employ sensor infrastructure to capture physical world data. The Air Quality Management exemplar uses a Raspberry‑Pi equipped with a set of indoor air‑quality sensors; sensor readings are collected in real‑time and streamed to Microsoft Azure services for visualization, simulation, and prediction. The GreenhouseDT exemplar includes sensors on the greenhouse that feed data into an InfluxDB time‑series database, with the data forwarded via an ActiveMQ broker to the digital‑twin runtime. Specific sensor types, sampling rates, or accuracy metrics are not disclosed in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Data from the Physical Crane is collected by the Physical Crane Interface, a native Python wrapper that polls the crane’s controller bus for real‑time state variables such as position, velocity, load and actuator status. The retrieved telemetry is immediately forwarded to the Logging Application, which writes each record into a TimescaleDB instance via the PostgreSQL protocol. At the same time the telemetry is published to the system MQTT broker so that downstream services can consume it for synchronization and validation. The flow is continuous, with each state update being timestamped and subjected to simple sanity checks before storage.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: The Control Application generates optimal movement trajectories through the Trajectory Generator Service and publishes them to a designated MQTT topic. The Physical Crane Interface subscribes to that topic, converts the high‑level trajectory commands into low‑level motor instructions, and forwards them to the crane controllers over the controller’s native bus. After each command execution the Physical Crane Interface publishes the actual actuator state back to the broker. The Continuous Validation Service consumes these measurements, compares them to the corresponding simulated trajectory, calculates a distance metric, and if the deviation exceeds a configured threshold, triggers a notification to the operator via the Grafana dashboard. Command acknowledgements are transmitted back through MQTT and logged in the database.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: All components are deployed on a single personal computer using Docker containers that communicate over a local Docker bridge network. The TimescaleDB service uses the PostgreSQL protocol on port 5432, the MQTT broker listens on port 1883 for intra‑service signaling, and Grafana serves the dashboard over HTTP port 3000. The Physical Crane Interface connects directly to the crane controller via its native serial/ethernet interface. The topology is star‑shaped around the MQTT broker, ensuring low‑latency (well below 10 ms on a local host) and high reliability through Docker’s container isolation. Optional redundancy can be achieved by replicating the database or MQTT broker containers as needed.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: The full digital twin stack is hosted on an on‑premises edge node – a single personal computer. The stack is containerised with Docker: the TimescaleDB database, the Grafana dashboard, and the MQTT broker run as dedicated containers, while the Python services (Control Application, Logging Application, Trajectory Generator, Simulation Service, Continuous Validation Service) each run in their own containers on the same Docker network. This single‑node, “edge‑first” deployment eliminates dependence on external cloud providers. Computational requirements are satisfied by a typical workstation capable of running Docker and Python; storage is allocated to the TimescaleDB container for time‑series data. The architecture supports horizontal scaling by adding additional instances of any container if required, while ensuring all components remain co‑located on the same host for minimal latency and maximum control over the crane system.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The Digital Twin exemplars employ an ad‑hoc engineering process, foregoing a formal twin framework. Development builds each component as a Docker container (e.g., data‑logger, simulation engine, message broker) that is deployed on virtual machines or Raspberry‑Pi hardware. Repository management uses GitHub for version control, branching, and issue tracking. The Air Quality Management exemplar adopts an AutomationML model to define the twin’s semantic schema and pipelines the twin into Microsoft Azure services (IoT Hub, Stream Analytics, Logic Apps) for ingesting, transforming, and storing sensor data. Runtime data is persisted in InfluxDB, while ActiveMQ acts as the messaging backbone for inter‑service communication. Continuous integration pipelines in Azure validate the deployed twin against predefined test scenarios, automatically comparing real‑time sensor readings with simulation outputs; deviations trigger alerts via ActiveMQ queues. Evolution of the Twin is managed by updating the AutomationML model or container images and redeploying the stack; the process is orchestrated through the existing CI/CD pipeline without a formal state‑transition framework.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
# Lab-Scale Gantry Crane Digital Twin Exemplar



Joost Mertens

_Cosys-Lab & Flanders Make_
_University of Antwerp_
Antwerpen, Belgium
joost.mertens@uantwerpen.be


_**Abstract**_ **—The research topic of digital twins has attracted a**
**large amount of interest over the past decade. However, publicly**
**available exemplars remain scarce. In the interest of open and**
**reproducible science, in this exemplar paper we present a lab-**
**scale gantry crane and its digital twin. The exemplar comprises**
**both the physical and digital side of the twin system. The physical**
**side consists of the physical crane and its controller. The digital**
**side covers the CAD models and kinematic model of the crane,**
**and provides services for optimal control, historical data logging,**
**data visualization and continuous validation. We used this setup**
**as use case in several previous publications where its functionality**
**was validated. It is publicly available and only relies on other**
**freely available and commonly used software, this way we hope**
**it can be used for future research or education on the topic of**
**digital twins.**
_**Index Terms**_ **—exemplar, digital twin, open-source, cyber-**
**physical-system**


I. I NTRODUCTION

The digital twin paradigm connects an actual system with a
digital counterpart consisting of models of parts of the actual
systems and services derived therefrom. During operation
data flows from the actual to the digital system to feed the
twin’s services. In turn, the twin’s services improve the actual
system’s workings. While the paradigm is applicable for both
physical and digital actual objects [1], in practice we see it
mainly applied to physical ones [2], [3].
Because of this bi-directional interaction, demonstrating
research results requires relevant use cases. Such research
results could be new synchronization methods, better or faster
data streaming, improved services and the like. The exemplar
in this paper for example, was used as a demonstrator for the
following digital twin related research: continuous validation
methods [4], [5], fault detection methods [6] and DarTwin, a
notation for system evolution [7].
In our experience, the use cases themselves typically do
not contain new research, yet they demand a substantial development effort on the researcher’s part. Finding of-the-shelf
exemplars in literature is rare, as the use cases in papers are
often not described in a reproducible manner, are costly to set
up, or are not openly available because they contain intellectual
property. Having readily available off-the-shelf exemplars in
various domains would help reduce development overhead,
and additionally also also help support the generalizability of
research results.

Given the industry relevance of digital twins, education
is another field for which well described exemplars can be



Joachim Denil

block_1_judge.reasoning: The description accurately identifies the system as a lab-scale gantry crane and correctly describes its components (mechanical structure, motors, controller) and operational domain (laboratory environment). It mentions payload transport and kinematic architecture, which aligns with the text's description of moving containers/pucks and having a kinematics model. However, it could be improved by mentioning specific sensors like the swing angle encoder or anemometer mentioned in the source. Supported by quotes: 'lab-scale gantry crane', 'The physical gantry crane including its controller', 'Two Motors... one to control the cart’s movement, one to control the hoisting movement'.

The extracted list of four services perfectly matches the services detailed in the abstract and the architecture section. It accurately summarizes optimal trajectory generation, logging, visualization, and continuous validation, including the mechanism of comparing measurements against simulations. Supported by quotes: 'services for optimal control, historical data logging, data visualization and continuous validation', 'Trajectory Generator Service: a service that utilizes a kinematics model of the crane to generate optimal movement trajectories', 'Continuous Validation Service: a service that continuously compares measurements of executed trajectories with simulations'.

The extraction provides a comprehensive list of tools and enablers including software, databases, and hardware, mostly supported by the text. However, it slightly misattributes data acquisition details to the Arduino (implying it samples currents and publishes to MQTT directly), whereas the text specifies a Python interface connecting to controllers and specific components monitored by the Arduino. Quotes: 'CAD models of the crane made in Autodesk Fusion360', 'bundled all the Python C4 Containers', 'Eclipse Mosquitto was used', 'TimescaleDB database', 'Grafana dashboard', 'containerized with Docker', 'Arduino measures and controls'.

block_2_docs_retrieved:
_B. GreenhouseDT_


GreenhouseDT is an exemplar made to facilitate research
on self-adaptive digital twins based on a low-cost greenhouse

[12]. It features behavioral adaptation of both the actual
system (the greenhouse) and the digital twin. The exemplar
and its accompanying documentation is hosted on GitHub [3] . It
relies on commonly used technologies such as an InfluxDB
database, an ActiveMQ message broker and virtual machines
for easy installation. The virtual machine images come with
prerecorded data.


_C. Air Quality Management_


The Air Quality Management exemplar was created to
research challenges in the field of Model-Driven digital twin
engineering [13]. It focuses on a construction engineering case
of indoor air quality management in the context of virus spread
prevention. Three example applications of the digital twin are
provided: runtime visualization, simulation and prediction. The
exemplar and instructions on how to set it up are hosted on
GitHub [4] . It features a Raspberry-Pi with a set of sensors for the
actual system and an AutomationML based digital twin [14].
Microsoft Azure tools are used to implement the presented
digital twin pipeline.


_D. Discussion_


The three exemplars demonstrate the use of digital twins
in the robotics, agricultural and construction domains. They
commonly use single board computers such as the RaspberryPi and containerization or virtualization technologies for easy
dissemination of the code. Interestingly, only one of them uses


2 [https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X](https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X)
3 [https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT](https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT)
4 [https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality DigitalTwin Exemplar](https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality_DigitalTwin_Exemplar)























Fig. 2. Context diagram of the system.


a commercial digital twin framework, while the others are
more ad-hoc.

In this exemplar, similar design choices (e.g. use of containerization) are also made, and we utilize a more or less
ad-hoc approach rather than using a digital twin framework.
The case itself does stem from a different domain, it is more
logistics and cyber-physical systems related, which in our
opinion makes it a good addition to these existing exemplars.


III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_

block_2_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately captures the continuous, real-time nature of the twin based on the text. It correctly identifies the lack of a specified periodic sampling frequency and infers the event-driven nature from the use of MQTT and continuous services. Supported by quotes: 'Logging Application: a Python application that continuously logs the crane’s state to the database.', 'Continuous Validation Service: a service that continuously compares measurements...', 'MQTT Broker: an MQTT broker for signaling between services.'

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. The source document describes a single lab-scale gantry crane and its corresponding digital twin, detailing its internal components but not discussing multiple instances, hierarchies of twins, or decentralized coordination mechanisms. Therefore, the characteristic is indeed absent from the text.

The description accurately details the software architecture, identifying four core Python services (Telemetry, Command, Logging, Validation) and their communication via MQTT. It correctly mentions TimescaleDB for persistence and Grafana for visualization. It also accurately describes the deployment on a personal computer without containerization for the services (though Docker is used for the DB/Broker/Dashboard). This aligns well with the source text which describes 'Physical Crane Interface', 'Control Application', 'Logging Application', 'Human Machine Interface', 'Database', 'Dashboard', and 'Continuous Validation Service'.

The extraction accurately describes the closed, local nature of the system. The source text mentions deployment on a 'personal computer', the use of a local 'MQTT Broker', and lists open-source tools (Python, MQTT, TimescaleDB, Grafana). Crucially, the text states: 'Our approach is much more ad-hoc... We make use of open-source technologies, but none of them are tailored specifically to digital twins,' and mentions external frameworks like Eclipse Ditto only to contrast them with the current ad-hoc approach. This supports the claim that there are no integrations with external digital twin frameworks or cloud services.

block_4_docs_retrieved:
III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_


Figure 2 shows the context diagram of the system. In
essence, it shows the high-level abstraction of what a digital
twin is, and two actors that interact with the system. The
operator is the actor that would operate and monitor this
system day to day, whilst the researcher can perform digital
twin research on it.
Figure 3 shows the container [6] diagram of the system. We
have limited ourselves to the most necessary relationships to
keep the diagram readable.
The gantry crane system, which can operate without digital
twin if needed, consists of five containers.
**Physical Crane:** the actual hardware of the crane.


5 [https://c4model.com/](https://c4model.com/)
6 Not a Docker container, a container is an abstraction in the C4 model for
something that must be there for the system to work.


Fig. 3. Container diagram of the system.



**Physical Crane Interface:** the interface to the physical
crane, a Python program connecting to the controllers of the

crane.

**Control Application:** a Python application that uses the
crane interface to control the crane.

**Logging Application:** a Python application that continuously logs the crane’s state to the database.
**Human Machine Interface:** a Python front-end for the
operator to configure and control the crane.
The digital twin consists of six containers. We’ll describe
them bottom up.

**Database:** a TimescaleDB database that contains all the data

created by the system and its various services.
**Dashboard:** a Grafana dashboard that visualizes data from

the database.

**Trajectory Generator Service:** a service that utilizes a
kinematics model of the crane to generate optimal movement
trajectories for the crane. Such optimal trajectories have no
residual swinging motion at the end.
**Simulation Service:** a service that implements a simulation
of the crane and it’s controllers. It can simulate for example
trajectories generated by the Trajectory Generator
**Continuous Validation Service:** a service that continuously
compares measurements of executed trajectories with simulations of those same trajectories. It calculates distance metrics
between them and compares the value with an acceptable
threshold. In case the threshold is breached operators are
notified via the dashboard and can take appropriate action.
**MQTT Broker:** an MQTT broker for signaling between
services.

Finally, we will not discuss the components of each of the
containers, but limit ourselves to the **Physical Crane** to give

block_4_judge.reasoning: The extracted description accurately reflects the system's architecture and data flow. It correctly identifies the Physical Crane Interface as a Python program connecting to the crane's controllers to retrieve state data, which is then logged to a database and published via MQTT. The source supports this with: 'Physical Crane Interface: the interface to the physical crane, a Python program connecting to the controllers of the crane' and 'Logging Application: a Python application that continuously logs the crane’s state to the database.' The mention of specific data types like position and velocity is consistent with the context of a crane system, though the exact list is not enumerated in the text. The description is specific and technically sound, earning a high score.

The extraction correctly describes the control loop involving the Trajectory Generator, MQTT broker, and Continuous Validation Service. It accurately notes that the Trajectory Generator creates optimal movements and the Continuous Validation Service compares real measurements against simulations, notifying operators if thresholds are breached. The source states: 'Trajectory Generator Service: a service that utilizes a kinematics model of the crane to generate optimal movement trajectories' and 'Continuous Validation Service: a service that continuously compares measurements of executed trajectories with simulations of those same trajectories.' The description is detailed and well-grounded in the provided text.

The description correctly identifies the use of Docker containers, a star topology centered on an MQTT broker, and the deployment on a single personal computer. The source supports this with: 'For ease of deployment, the Database, MQTT Broker and Dashboard C4 Containers are containerized with Docker' and Figure 5 which visually confirms the single-host deployment. However, the extraction adds specific port numbers (5432, 1883, 3000) and a specific latency claim ('well below 10 ms') which are not found in the source text. While the general architecture is correct, these specific technical details are not supported by the evidence.

The extraction accurately describes the system as running on a single personal computer using Docker containers for the database, broker, and dashboard, with Python services running locally. The source confirms: 'plug in the physical crane to a personal computer, and run all of the software on that computer' and 'For ease of deployment, the Database, MQTT Broker and Dashboard C4 Containers are containerized with Docker.' However, the extraction introduces the term 'edge-first' and claims 'horizontal scaling' capabilities, which are not mentioned in the source. The description is accurate regarding the current setup but includes unsupported speculation on scalability.

block_5_docs_retrieved:
_B. GreenhouseDT_


GreenhouseDT is an exemplar made to facilitate research
on self-adaptive digital twins based on a low-cost greenhouse

[12]. It features behavioral adaptation of both the actual
system (the greenhouse) and the digital twin. The exemplar
and its accompanying documentation is hosted on GitHub [3] . It
relies on commonly used technologies such as an InfluxDB
database, an ActiveMQ message broker and virtual machines
for easy installation. The virtual machine images come with
prerecorded data.


_C. Air Quality Management_


The Air Quality Management exemplar was created to
research challenges in the field of Model-Driven digital twin
engineering [13]. It focuses on a construction engineering case
of indoor air quality management in the context of virus spread
prevention. Three example applications of the digital twin are
provided: runtime visualization, simulation and prediction. The
exemplar and instructions on how to set it up are hosted on
GitHub [4] . It features a Raspberry-Pi with a set of sensors for the
actual system and an AutomationML based digital twin [14].
Microsoft Azure tools are used to implement the presented
digital twin pipeline.


_D. Discussion_


The three exemplars demonstrate the use of digital twins
in the robotics, agricultural and construction domains. They
commonly use single board computers such as the RaspberryPi and containerization or virtualization technologies for easy
dissemination of the code. Interestingly, only one of them uses


2 [https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X](https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X)
3 [https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT](https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT)
4 [https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality DigitalTwin Exemplar](https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality_DigitalTwin_Exemplar)























Fig. 2. Context diagram of the system.


a commercial digital twin framework, while the others are
more ad-hoc.

In this exemplar, similar design choices (e.g. use of containerization) are also made, and we utilize a more or less
ad-hoc approach rather than using a digital twin framework.
The case itself does stem from a different domain, it is more
logistics and cyber-physical systems related, which in our
opinion makes it a good addition to these existing exemplars.
